items.q: Why is a food web a better model than a food chain?
items.correct: A food chain shows one linear path; a food web shows the many interconnected feeding relationships in reality, so it better represents how species depend on multiple food sources.
items.misconception: A food web is the same as a food chain, just drawn differently.
items.random_wrong: A food web shows what spiders eat and a food chain shows what other animals eat.
items.why: Most species have multiple predators and prey. A linear chain is a simplification — the web captures redundancy and resilience. Removing one species rarely collapses a real ecosystem.

items.q: How do humans affect the carbon cycle?
items.correct: By burning fossil fuels (releasing stored geological carbon to the atmosphere) and deforestation (reducing the biosphere store and releasing carbon).
items.misconception: Humans only affect the carbon cycle by breathing out CO2.
items.random_wrong: Humans affect the carbon cycle by eating plants.
items.why: Human respiration is balanced (we eat carbon that plants captured). The disruption is shifting carbon from long-term geological stores into the active atmospheric cycle.

items.q: What is the difference between GDP and GNI?
items.correct: GDP measures all economic activity within a country's borders; GNI adds income from citizens and companies abroad and subtracts income earned by foreigners inside — capturing national income flows.
items.misconception: GDP and GNI are the same — just measured by different organisations.
items.random_wrong: GDP is for the government and GNI is for individuals.
items.why: For countries with large diasporas (e.g. the Philippines), GNI is higher than GDP due to remittances. For TNC-host countries, GNI may be lower than GDP. The distinction matters for development comparison.
Correct answer: Human Development Index. “High Development Indicator” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.
The answer examiners want is: Norway. “UK” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: How many stages in Rostow's model?
items.why: Exact answer: 5 stages. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
